Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd try to sit down and study when I felt like it, but it never stuck to any schedule. Sometimes I'd spend hours watching videos on the LMS, other times I'd go days without opening anything. With 17 courses going on, I just kind of bounced around between whatever felt most urgent or least overwhelming at the moment.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely not regular. I'd cram when deadlines were coming up and then basically disappear from studying for a while after. Some weeks I'd put in decent hours, other weeks almost nothing. It really depended on my anxiety levels and whether I could even focus that day.
Q3: Usually panic about an assignment due soon would get me to log in. Or sometimes I'd just feel restless and scroll through the course materials without really absorbing anything. Notifications helped remind me things existed, but half the time I'd see them and still not log in until later. It was rarely a planned decision.
Q4: For a little while in the middle of the semester, I got into a groove of checking the LMS most mornings with my coffee. It felt almost normal, like checking social media. But that routine fell apart pretty quickly when assignments piled up and I started feeling overwhelmed. After that, logging in felt more like a chore than a habit.
Q5: The videos were honestly the most useful for me because I could rewatch parts I didn't get the first time. I'm a hands-on learner, so just reading stuff doesn't stick, but seeing someone work through problems helped. The discussion posts were okay too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that made more sense than the lectures.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and the longer articles. I'd open them, see walls of text, and just close them again. Without internet at home, I couldn't always access things when I wanted to, so I'd tell myself I'd read them later and then never did. The optional quizzes also got skipped a lot because they felt pointless if they weren't graded.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to what I'd be tested on or if it was hands-on somehow. Like practice problems or interactive stuff where I could actually do something instead of just read. If it felt too abstract or disconnected from assignments, I'd lose interest fast and move on to something else.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could ignore, so I usually did. Required ones at least had the pressure of a grade attached, which sometimes was the only thing that got me to do them. But even required stuff, if it wasn't weighted heavily, I'd sometimes skip it. The optional ones just never felt urgent enough to prioritize.
Q9: Yeah, a lot actually. I'd download videos and PDFs when I could access wifi at the library, then study from those at home. I also had a notebook where I'd write out concepts by hand because that helps me remember better. None of that would show up anywhere in the system, but it was real studying.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going through my handwritten notes or practicing problems on paper, and the LMS would show nothing. Or I'd download a lecture, watch it three times offline, and it would only register as one view. My actual engagement was way higher than what the system could track.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download stuff once and then use it repeatedly offline. Like, I'd grab a whole week's worth of materials in one session at the library, then work through them slowly at home over several days. The LMS probably thought I was inactive, but I was actually studying those materials pretty intensely.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. My anxiety spiked and I just couldn't focus on anything. I'd sit down to study and my mind would go blank or race with worries about failing. The workload from all my courses felt crushing, and I started avoiding the LMS because even looking at it stressed me out.
Q13: Absolutely. The fatigue was real, especially without good resources at home. I'd get mentally exhausted trying to keep up with everything, and the LMS would be the first thing I'd neglect. Competing responsibilities like helping out at home also ate into my study time. Confusion about course material made me avoid it instead of seeking help.
Q14: Oh, for sure. I'd log in and just scroll through things without really reading or thinking about them. Sometimes I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about exams or just zoning out. I was technically 'active' but not actually learning anything meaningful in those moments.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important but the anxiety would make me avoid it. It's like the more important something was, the scarier it felt to start. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd feel the same way. It's a terrible cycle I couldn't break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd procrastinate until the last possible moment, then rush through everything in a panic. The LMS would show this burst of activity right before due dates. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have submitted anything at all.
Q17: When I got feedback, it sometimes motivated me briefly, especially if it was encouraging. But negative feedback or bad grades made my anxiety worse and made me want to avoid the course entirely. The CBT sessions helped me process that a bit better, but it was still hard not to take criticism personally and shut down.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with so many modules and requirements across all my courses. I never felt like I had a clear path to follow. When things were broken into smaller chunks it helped, but often it just felt like an endless list of tasks I'd never complete. That made me disengage more.
Q19: Having videos instead of just readings made things easier for me since I learn better that way. But the sheer amount of content made engagement harder. Also, not having consistent internet access meant I couldn't participate in discussions or access materials when I actually had time to study, which was frustrating.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me, honestly. You're logging in, watching stuff, clicking around, but the actual assignments feel too hard or too anxiety-inducing to complete. Or you're trying to understand the material but struggling so much that you can't produce anything worth submitting. Activity doesn't equal understanding.
Q21: That's not really my experience since I had high activity but poor performance. But I can see how someone who studies efficiently offline could pull that off. Maybe they're just better at focusing and don't need to spend as much time in the system. Or they have better resources and support outside the LMS.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or if you were just going through the motions. They miss all the offline studying, the mental state you were in, the anxiety that made you avoid things. It's just surface-level data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting real effort into understanding it, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's when you're mentally present and curious, not just physically going through motions. For me, true engagement has been rare this semester because my anxiety keeps pulling me away.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was actually trying despite failing, or how much my anxiety affected everything. It can't capture the hours I spent studying offline or the emotional toll of feeling like I'm drowning in coursework. It just sees numbers and clicks, not the person behind them struggling to keep going.
